coredata.dc:title: Study on the fluorescence properties of carbon dots prepared via combustion process
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shiwang
Zhang, Liangxing
Huang, Linsen
Zheng, Gang
Zhang, Pei
Jin, Yong
Jiao, Zhifeng
Sun, Xiaosong
coredata.dc:description: The unique properties of carbon dots make it have wide potential applications. The combustion process is, thus far, the most representative and common technological path. In this very paper, carbon dots were prepared by burning flammable organic materials such as ethanol, n-butanol, domestic candle and benzene. A series of characterizations were carried out to reveal the structure and photo luminescence of the prepared carbon dots. It is found that the size of carbon dots was at 2–4 nm. More than half disordered graphitic phase was involved in carbon dots. With the length of carbon chain of the precursors grew, the content of disordered graphitic phase has increased. FT-IR revealed that there were C˭C bonds and oxygen containing group on the surface of carbon dots. Different precursors could change the surface groups and surface states of CDs slightly. As one dispersed the prepared carbon dots in the organic solvents, it is found that there would be the dependent photo fluorescence emission bands excited with the UV light of 330–400 nm. This dependent photo fluorescence emission bands relied on the length of carbon chain of the precursors, the aromatic framework in the carbon dots. The emission spectrum of carbon dots prepared with ethanol and n-butanol had good stability and can be used in a variety of solvent systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Combustion method
Carbon dots
Fluorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical TEM images of CDs: (a) low magnified image and (b) high magnified image. The inset of (b) gives the high resolution image CDs. The separation of the fringes is 0.32 nm.
FT-IR spectra of CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3 and CDs-4.
UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs in (a) acetone, (b) ethanol, (c) benzene and (d) petroleum ether.
(a) Raman spectra of CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3 and CDs-4, (b) fitted Raman spectrum with Gaussian functions.
PL spectra (λex= 330 nm) of CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3 and CDs-4 dispersed in (a) acetone, (b) ethanol, (c) benzene and (d) petroleum ether.

head.ce:title: Study on the fluorescence properties of carbon dots prepared via combustion process

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Sichuan University, No. 24 South Section 1, Yihuan Road, Chengdu, Sichuan, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Sichuan University,

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The unique properties of carbon dots make it have wide potential applications. The combustion process is, thus far, the most representative and common technological path. In this very paper, carbon dots were prepared by burning flammable organic materials such as ethanol, n-butanol, domestic candle and benzene. A series of characterizations were carried out to reveal the structure and photo luminescence of the prepared carbon dots. It is found that the size of carbon dots was at 2–4 nm. More than half disordered graphitic phase was involved in carbon dots. With the length of carbon chain of the precursors grew, the content of disordered graphitic phase has increased. FT-IR revealed that there were C˭C bonds and oxygen containing group on the surface of carbon dots. Different precursors could change the surface groups and surface states of CDs slightly. As one dispersed the prepared carbon dots in the organic solvents, it is found that there would be the dependent photo fluorescence emission bands excited with the UV light of 330–400 nm. This dependent photo fluorescence emission bands relied on the length of carbon chain of the precursors, the aromatic framework in the carbon dots. The emission spectrum of carbon dots prepared with ethanol and n-butanol had good stability and can be used in a variety of solvent systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a zero-dimensional spherical allotrope of carbon, was accidently invented in the procedure of purification of the soot, it was the side-product of the arc-discharge process to prepare carbon nanotubes [1,2]. Similar to the other advanced carbon materials, carbon nanotube, graphene and fullerene, for instance, CDs has soon made the fine figure and become the research focus of the science and technology community [3–5]. Since its discovery, one has placed great expectation on the applications of CDs, therefore, many approaches have been conducted to inexpensively prepare CDs. Laser ablation process, hydrothermal process and combustion process is the representative of the commonly used technological paths [6–16].

CDs possesses many notable properties, the fluorescence, biocompatibility, low toxicity, high thermal stability, and chemically inert, for instance [3,4,17–20]. One significant feature is that hydrothermally prepared CDs with citric acid and ethylenediamine is so hydrophilic that it can fully disperse in water. Whereas the soot, CDs produced by combusting domestic candle or other flammable organic materials, is poorly water dispersible. Hence, it is necessary for the soot to be refluxed with oxygen acid for good water dispersity [13,16]. Even so, the combustion-based approach is still a simple and straightforward and attracts much attention [3,13,15].

On the other hand, one has been deeply impressed at the intrinsic photo luminescence of CDs. The luminescence of CDs is the most attractive and worth expecting feature, which gives the high potential in biological labeling, bioimaging, and other different optoelectronic device applications [21–29]. However, the luminescence of CDs is poorly studied and the understanding of which is far from sufficient. For example, the finding about the microstructure and surface ligands remains unclear and details of the organic passivation are not sufficient, yet, to aid understanding of the surface states beneficial for light emission. Furthermore, though the effect between CDs and solvent molecules would benefit the fluorescence emission, it is still far from clear. In the pioneer studies, Xu and colleges reported that the soot could be converted into hydrophilic and photo-luminescence by means of a refluxing procedure of 5 M HNO3 for 12 h to oxidize the surface of the soot to make it negatively charged [13]. Ray and colleges added precipitants and extracted the supernatant after the refluxing procedure of oxygen acid, they found that the strongest fluorescence emission was at 520 nm with the excitation wavelength of 450 nm [13] and the quantum yield was size-dependent: The smaller nanoparticle had the higher fluorescent emission efficiency. On the other hand, the soot is most probably hydrophobic. Thus less attention to the soot with poor water dispersity was paid than water dispersible carbon dot.

Until 2015 Kumar and colleges did not report the photo-luminescence of the lamp soot in 10 organic solvents [15]. They pointed out that the lamp soot emitted characteristic fluorescence in different solvents; and the lamp soot had a significant fluorescence effect in organic solvents even if it was not functionalized yet. Though they presented the emission spectra of lamp soot, they frankly indicated that the source of luminescence was not clearly understood yet. They thought that the polycyclic aromatic compounds might be the source of fluorescence. The origin of the fluorescence from CDs might be, to some extent, the induced emissive trap states by the organic solvent or the polycyclic aromatic compounds in lamp soot. It is well known that the different precursors used in the hydrothermal method will donate different functional groups to CDs. Thus, the soot prepared with so different precursors that they might give different fluorescence. To our best knowledge, there seems no research focusing on this topic.

In this paper, we have presented the structure and photo-luminescent property of the soot prepared with four flammable carbonaceous precursors, that is, ethanol, n-butanol, domestic candle and benzene. These precursors are different either from the length of carbon chain or carbon aromatic framework. Thus, the characterization of the soot prepared can tell the impact of carbon chain or the aromatic framework on the structure and luminescence of the soot. At the same time, we have demonstrated the fluorescence of four kinds of soot in four representative organic solvents, acetone, ethanol, benzene and petroleum ether, for instance, the polarity of which are 5.4, 4.3, 3.0 and 0.01, respectively. Comparing with other three solvents, it is sure that the solvent effect between the soot and benzene (as solvent) may strongly affect the fluorescence of carbon dots.

The chemical reagents, ethanol, n-butanol, acetone, benzene and petroleum ether, were commercially purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Agent Company Ltd, while the domestic candle was bought in the store. The flammable organic solvent were analytical grade and used without further purification. The collected soot was characterized with transmission electron microscopy (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN), photo-luminescent spectroscopy (HORIBA, ScientificFluoomax-4), UV–vis spectroscopy (UNICO, UV2102PC), Fourier transition infrared spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Nicolet 6700) and Raman spectroscopy (HORIBA, LabRAM HR).

The soot was prepared by burning the flammable organic solvents in the air. In a typical burning procedure, 10 ml ethanol was poured into a crucible and burned in the air. To collect the soot, a beaker was put 10 cm over the flame up-side down. The soot was scratched out with a spatula and stored in bottles. The collected soot was named after CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3 and CDs-4 for the soot prepared with ethanol, n-butanol, domestic candle and benzene, respectively.

The preparation process of the dispersion system was: Put 2 mg of soot into 100 ml of solvent, then the mixture was stirred for 30 min and followed 2 h sonication. The solicited solution was then centrifuged (5000 rpm) for 3 min to remove the undispersed soot. The light-brown supernatant was collect for further characterization.

It is found that all of the soot was somewhat hydrophobic and slightly dispersible in the four representative organic solvents. The four organic solvents containing the CDs prepared had obvious photo-luminescence irradiated with UV lamp (λ = 255 nm), Fig. 1S. Fig. 1 presents the typical TEM images of CDs-1. One can find that, Fig. 1(a), the size of CDs is 2–4 nm. According to HR-TEM, the interlayer spacing of CDs is about 0.32 nm, which is close to that of (002) planes of graphite. The TEM images of the other CDs are presented in Fig. S2.

Fig. 2 illustrates the FT-IR spectra of four kinds of CDs. The bands around at 3420 cm−1 are attributed to the O–H stretching vibration, both the bands at 2922 cm-1 and 2853 cm−1 are attributed to C–H stretching deformations. At the same time, one can see that there is a weak absorption band at 3052 cm-1 for CDs-2, CDs-3 and CDs-4, which corresponds to the C˭C–H stretching vibration. One believes that in the burning procedure the flammable materials with long carbon chains would implant some unsaturated groups onto the product [30,31]. The absorption bands at 1600 cm-1 and 1453 cm-1 tell that all CDs contain some aromatic frameworks. This result is consistent with the speculation given by Kumar and colleges [15]. The absorption at 1380 cm-1 of CDs-1 is typical –CH3 stretching band and that at 1710 cm-1 of CDs-1 gives the information about C˭O, probably attributed to the short chain precursor (ethanol). The hydroxy group in molecule would produce a lot of carbonyl group during combustion process, since the absorption band of CDs-2 (from butanol) at 1710 cm-1 is weak; and CDs-3 and CDs-4 have even not at all the absorption band at around 1700 cm-1. The C–O stretching bands of 1300–1100 cm−1 indicate that all CDs contain oxygen-containing groups. Several peaks at 750–880 cm-1 represent the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons framework and substitution group information. The results aforementioned indicate that some characteristic groups of precursors were reserved in the CDs, that is, there would be aromatic skeletons in the core of CDs, and alkyl group, and some oxygen-containing groups on the surface.

Fig. 3 illustrates the UV–vis absorption spectra of the prepared CDs in four different solvents. One may find that the absorption edge of each CDs is dependent on the solvent: Different CDs has the same absorption edge in the same solvent, but the same CDs has different absorption edge in different solvents. The absorption edges locates at 200 nm in both ethanol and petroleum ether, Fig. 3b and d, and it shifts to 280 nm in benzene and 320 nm in acetone, Fig. 3a and c, respectively. Meanwhile, it is worth to be noted that, when polar solvent, acetone, for instance, was used, the signals of fine structures are so vague that we have to see the broad absorption bands, Fig. 3a. Nevertheless, as the solvents with weaker polarity, ethanol and benzene, for instance, were used, some of the fine absorption bands of UV–vis spectra can be seen, Fig. 3b, c and d. Due to the conjugate effect in benzene ring, the fine structures observed in Fig. 3c are different from those in Fig. 3b. Petroleum ether, a nonpolar solvent, can display abundant fine absorption bands of the UV–vis spectra of the prepared CDs, Fig. 3d. In Fig. 3b and d, two distinct absorption peaks at 220 nm and 270 nm can be observed. It is believed that the one at 220 nm is Information of the physical size of the graphitic structure, and the other one at 270 nm is the π-π* transition [15,32]. Besides the absorption bands at 220 nm and 270 nm, there is an additional absorption band of n-π* transition can be seen at 315 nm in Fig. 3b [32]. The domestic candle, the dominant component of which is paraffin, is composed of many kinds of linear alkanes. The UV–vis spectrum of CDs prepared with domestic candle has a large and wide absorption plateaus in Fig. 3d. The similar UV–vis spectra of CDs prepared with different precursors indicate that these CDs have the same structure and energy states. This conclusion is consistent with the FT-IR spectra, in which one can see that there are C˭C, oxygen containing group and aromatic structures in all CDs.

Raman spectra of prepared CDs are shown in Fig. 4. There are two prominent bands located at 1340 cm-1 and 1590 cm-1, respective. The band at 1340 cm-1, D band, corresponds to A1g mode of the disordered graphite, while the band at 1590 cm-1, G band, is assigned to the E2g mode and related to the vibrations of sp2 bond in CDs. The intensity ratio of the D band to G band of CDs-1 is I (D) /I (G)= 1.81 ± 0.02, which shows the disordered graphite is the dominant composition. The other results are 2.01 ± 0.04 (CDs-2), 2.28 ± 0.02 (CDs-3) and 2.42 ± 0.04 (CDs-4), are shown in Fig. S3. Fitting results proves that the disordered graphite is the major part in all CDs. In addition, as the length of carbon chain of precursor increased, the case of domestic candle, or unsaturated degree increased, the case of benzene, the disordered graphite phase in CDs had an increasing tendency, to some extent.

Although the prepared CDs have the similar structures, they would still possess different steady-state photo-luminescent spectra. Fig. S4 shows the excitation spectra of CDs dispersed in ethanol. It is found that the effective excitation wavelength covers the range from 280 nm to 380 nm. The complicated excitation spectra predict that CDs has many electron transition configuration states in organic solvents. Fig. 5 gives the photo-luminescence spectra of the four prepared CDs dispersed in acetone, ethanol, benzene and petroleum ether, respectively. Unlike the photo-luminescence spectrum of CDs prepared by hydrothermal method, which is a Gaussian-like peak, there are many distinct peaks in the photo-luminescence spectra of CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3, moreover, the photo-luminescence spectra of CDs-4 is a broad band.

One can see that, Fig. 5, the photo-luminescence spectrum of CDs-4 is quite different from those of CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3. The fluorescence emission band of CDs-4 covers the range from 380 to 520 nm with the maximum peak at 460 nm. The position of the maximum peak has no significant change with decreasing the solvent polarity. However with decreasing the polarity there emerged an emission shoulder centred at 390 nm and some fine emission peaks.

Regarding the CDs prepared with different carbon sources, their photo-luminescence spectra are much more complicated. The foremost emission peaks of CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3 cover the range from 450 to 550 nm, the maximum intensity of which is around 460 nm. The maximum intensity of the spectrum of CDs-4 is also at 460 nm. This coincidence shows that CDs prepared by combustion process have similar carbon framework structure and, thus, possess the same primary emissive states. When CDs were dispersed in acetone, fine emission peaks emerged, Fig. 5a. Interestingly, when the polarity of the solvent was decreased, fine information is obscured, Figs. 5b and 5c. In general, the details of fine UV–vis absorption bands can be seen in the organic solvent with weak polarity, while the details of fine transition bands can be seen in the organic solvent with strong polarity.

Of course, not only the polarity of the solvent will affect the photo-luminescence spectra, but the length of carbon chain of the precursors can affect the photo-luminescence spectra too. For CDs from the precursors with short carbon chain (CDs-1), its fluorescence emission is stronger at longer wavelength (530 nm). Whereas, the CDs derived from those with longcarbon-chain (CDs-3) has more complex emission peaks at 410–440 nm. This indicates that long carbon chain materials will generate new emission states after combustion.

The CDs were prepared via the combustion process with flammable organic materials such as ethanol, n-butanol, domestic candle and benzene. Though the used precursors were quite different, the prepared CDs possessed similar aromatic framework, which gave them similar photo-luminescence and UV–vis absorption features. The precursor with shorter carbon chain would implant methyl groups and carbonyl onto the CDs, giving rise to a more pronounced emission in the long wavelength; and that with a longer carbon chain or a cyclic carbon source would introduce more unsaturated bonds, thus, giving rise to a more complex emission in the short wavelength. According to the characterizations, it is found that the size of CDs was at 2–4 nm. More than half disordered graphitic phase was involved in CDs. With the length of carbon chain of the precursors grew, the content of disordered graphitic phase has increased. FT-IR revealed that the precursor would result the C˭C bonds and oxygen containing group in CDs, and the surface functional groups were also dependent on the precursors used. As one dispersed the prepared CDs in the organic solvents, it is found that there would be the dependent PL emission bands with the excitation of 330–400 nm. The emission band of prepared CDs was located at 430–550 nm. The dependent photo-fluorescence emission bands rely on the length of carbon chain of the precursors, the aromatic framework in the CDs. Moreover, the emission spectrum of CDs prepared with ethanol and n-butanol had good stability and can be used in a variety of solvent systems.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2018.10.086.
